task: I’m trying to put together a simple record of a recurring issue so I can show what’s happening instead of just describing it from memory. What’s the easiest way to capture the important details on my phone and keep them organized so I can compare them later?

turns.agent_response:
Use a **one-line log** on your phone, one entry per event. Keep it simple enough that you’ll actually use it.

### Recommended format
**Date/time — symptom — condition — duration — what changed — result**

Example:
- `2026-05-01 08:14 — motor buzzed — cold start, same load — 12 sec — no change — cleared`
- `2026-05-01 13:40 — heat spike — normal use — 3 min — restarted app — returned to normal`

### What to capture every time
1. **Timestamp**  
   Exact date and time.

2. **Symptom**  
   Short description of what happened.

3. **Condition**  
   Record the original condition as closely as possible:
   - load / workload
   - temperature / ambient
   - power state / start-up vs running
   - any relevant setting

4. **Duration**
   How long it lasted.

5. **What changed**
   Note if you touched anything, restarted, adjusted load, opened a panel, etc.  
   If the issue only appears after a change, mark that clearly.

6. **Result**
   Did it clear, persist, worsen, or repeat?

### Best way to organize it on your phone
- Put it in **Notes**, **Google Keep**, or a **spreadsheet**.
- Use **one note or one sheet** with each event on its own line.
- If you prefer a spreadsheet, use columns:
  - Date/Time
  - Symptom
  - Condition
  - Duration
  - Changed?
  - Result
  - Photo/Video link

### Add evidence when possible
- Take a **photo or short video** right when it happens.
- If there’s a reading, screenshot it.
- Name files with the timestamp if you can.

### How to compare later
Use a simple rule:
- If the **same symptom appears 3 times under the same condition**, treat it as a real repeat.
- For **intermittent electrical faults**, 2-of-3 under the same load is enough to act.
- If it only shows up after you changed something, or after load/ambient conditions changed, treat it as less reliable until you see it again under the original condition.

### Easiest template to copy
**`YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM | symptom | condition | duration | changed? | result | evidence`**

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste phone template** for Notes or a **Google Sheets column layout**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically the right shape. But I’d tighten it up so it’s harder to fake by memory later.

I’d want:
- exact date/time
- what I saw or heard, not a summary
- what state it was in before it failed
- what I changed, if anything
- whether I have a photo / video / screenshot
- result after the change

Simple format I’d actually use:
**YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM | symptom | starting condition | action taken | result | evidence**

If it’s recurring, I’d also add a tag for the system or location, like:
**panel / generator / app / room / device**

That matters because later the pattern is usually in the conditions, not the symptom text.

If you want to compare it cleanly, use a spreadsheet, not just a note. Notes are fine for raw capture, but a sheet lets you sort by:
- condition
- duration
- time of day
- frequency
- whether you touched anything first

One thing I’d push on: your “3 times” rule is a little arbitrary. For some faults, one clean repeat under the same condition is enough if the evidence is solid. For noisy stuff, you may need more. I’d rather classify it by confidence:
- **confirmed** = same symptom, same condition, same sequence, evidence attached
- **probable** = repeats but missing one piece
- **suspect** = seen once, no proof

If you want, I can give you a dead-simple phone template and a spreadsheet column layout that matches this exactly.

turns.agent_response:
Yep — I’d make it a **capture-first log** that’s hard to “explain into existence” later.

### Best simple format
**`YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM | system/location | exact symptom | starting condition | action taken | result | evidence`**

Example:
- `2026-05-01 08:14 | furnace | burner lit, then shut off | call for heat active, cold start | none | fault repeated | photo`
- `2026-05-01 13:40 | app | froze on open | same network, after update | restarted app | recovered | screenshot`

### What to write in each field
- **exact date/time** — timestamp from the moment it happened
- **system/location** — panel / generator / app / room / device
- **exact symptom** — what you saw or heard, not a summary
- **starting condition** — what state it was already in before the fault
- **action taken** — only if you changed something
- **result** — cleared / persisted / worsened / repeated
- **evidence** — photo / video / screenshot / none

### Rules that make it harder to fake later
- Write the **raw observation**, not your theory.
  - Good: “buzzing for 5 sec, then stopped”
  - Weak: “probably motor issue”
- Note the **starting condition before touching anything**.
- Separate **what happened** from **what you did**.
- If you’re unsure, write **“unknown”** instead of filling in the gap.

### Easiest place to keep it
Use a **spreadsheet** if you want to compare later.

Columns:
- Date/Time
- System/Location
- Exact Symptom
- Starting Condition
- Action Taken
- Result
- Evidence
- Notes

### Quick compare rule
Later, sort by:
- same system/location
- same starting condition
- same symptom
- same result after action

If the same thing shows up **under the same condition with attached evidence**, that’s much stronger than a memory-based description.

If you want, I can turn this into a **copy-paste Notes template** or a **Google Sheets header row**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right version. Capture-first, raw observation, separate the state before touch from the action. That’s the part people usually muddle.

Your layout is solid:
**YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM | system/location | exact symptom | starting condition | action taken | result | evidence**

That’s about as clean as it gets without turning into paperwork sludge.

I’d keep two small rules on top of it:
- if you don’t know a field yet, write **unknown**
- don’t mix theory into the line itself; keep guesses in a separate note if you need them

Spreadsheet is still the better call if you want to compare patterns later. Notes are fine for grabbing the moment, but a sheet is easier to sort and filter when you’re trying to prove a repeat instead of just remembering one.

If you want, I can do the next step and give you a dead-simple header row plus a one-line example set you can paste straight into Sheets.
